title: Alignment-Aware Decoding
abstract: Alignment of large language models remains a central challenge in natural language processing. Preference optimization has emerged as a popular and effective method for improving alignment, typically through training-time or prompt-based interventions. In this paper, we introduce alignment-aware decoding (AAD), a method to enhance model alignment directly at inference. Theoretically, AAD can be interpreted as implicit reward optimization, yet it requires no specialized training beyond the standard DPO setup. Empirically, AAD consistently outperforms strong baselines across diverse alignment benchmarks and model scales. Moreover, in data-constrained settings, AAD can produce high-quality synthetic data to improve alignment under standard decoding, providing a practical solution when labeled data is limited.
decision_comment:
This paper proposes Alignment-Aware Decoding (AAD), an inference-time method that uses the DPO model together with its SFT reference to steer decoding toward preference-aligned outputs. After reading the reviews, rebuttal, and follow-up discussion, I find the paper technically sound, clearly presented, and sufficiently novel. The strongest factors in favor of acceptance are that the method is simple and well motivated, and that the empirical case was substantially strengthened during rebuttal: the authors added results on stronger open DPO models, full fine-tuning settings, human evaluation, latency measurements, and additional iterative-DPO experiments. These additions resolved most of the initial concerns for three reviewers, leading to final recommendations of Accept / Weak Accept / Weak Accept.

The main remaining concern is the lack of evaluation on standard capability benchmarks such as MMLU or GSM8K, as emphasized by one reviewer. I agree this is a real limitation, and the paper should be explicit about the scope of its claims. That said, I do not view it as decisive here. AAD is a decoding method aimed at open-ended generation, and the paper now includes evaluation beyond reward-model scores, including human judgments and GPT-4-based pairwise evaluation across multiple model families, which meaningfully addresses concerns about reward over-optimization. The practical cost of requiring two model passes is also an important limitation, but the rebuttal quantified this overhead and clarified the deployment tradeoff.

Overall, the paper makes a useful and timely contribution on inference-time alignment. While some evaluation gaps remain, the evidence is strong enough to support acceptance, and I expect the method to be of interest to the ICML community.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes a decoding strategy called Alignment-Aware Decoding (AAD). This method leverages the implicit reward function in the optimization objective of DPO and treats it as an approximate token-level advantage function. During decoding, tokens that maximize this approximate token-level advantage are selected. Consequently, AAD is an inference-time method that operates during the decoding process and requires two large language models (LLMs): one trained with DPO and the model prior to DPO training. The paper evaluates AAD on the Llama3.2 and Qwen3 series of LLMs across several preference datasets, including HHRLHF and Skywork.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths
- AAD is a training-free approach and is easy to implement.
- The paper conducts extensive experiments to evaluate the effectiveness of AAD across multiple LLMs and preference datasets, and demonstrates that AAD can also be used to generate data for iterative DPO.

Weaknesses
- Although AAD consistently outperforms other baselines in the experimental results reported in the main text, these results are obtained using LLMs fine-tuned with LoRA. As shown in Table 5, which presents results for LLMs trained with full-parameter fine-tuning, the performance gap between AAD and the baselines narrows when full-parameter fine-tuning is used instead of LoRA. Consequently, AAD may be effective for relatively undertrained LLMs, while its effectiveness on stronger and well-trained LLMs remains unclear.
- AAD requires two LLMs, one of which must be trained with DPO. This requirement may limit the applicability of AAD in certain scenarios.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
- UltraChat is typically used as a supervised fine-tuning dataset rather than a preference dataset. Did the authors intend to refer to UltraFeedback instead of UltraChat?
- Since AAD is a training-free method, please evaluate it on well-trained open-source LLMs that have already been trained with DPO, such as OLMo-2-1124-7B-DPO and OLMo-2-1124-13B-DPO, and compare its performance with other baselines.
- Please repeat the experiments presented in Figure 4 under the full-parameter fine-tuning setting rather than LoRA, as the number of trainable parameters should scale with the size of the training data.
reviews.final_justification: The responses address several of my major concerns and increase my confidence in the validity and reliability of the proposed method. At this stage, I will adjust my overall recommendation.

reviews.summary: The key insight is that, under Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), the difference between the log probabilities of the DPO model and the original SFT model implicitly reflects a reward signal aligned with human preferences. Based on this observation, AAD modifies the decoding procedure. During generation, candidate tokens are first filtered by their probability under the DPO model, and then ranked according to this implicit reward signal derived from the DPO–SFT probability ratio. This encourages the model to generate responses that are more strongly preferred by the "reward" factor of the DPO model.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
## Strengths
Simple and elegant idea

One of the strongest aspects of the paper is the conceptual simplicity of the method. The authors leverage a theoretical property of Direct Preference Optimization (DPO): the log-probability ratio between the DPO model and the reference SFT model implicitly encodes a preference-aligned reward signal.

Using this observation, the paper proposes a decoding rule that directly incorporates this signal at inference time. The resulting method is intuitively motivated and easy to understand, which is a valuable property for alignment techniques.

## Weaknesses
The proposed method raises potential concerns regarding reward over-optimization. The evaluation primarily relies on reward models, which themselves serve as proxies for human preference. Since DPO training already encodes preferences learned from such proxy signals, the implicit reward used by AAD may ultimately reflect the same underlying objective used during evaluation.

As a result, AAD may be implicitly optimized to generate outputs that score highly under reward models rather than to improve genuine human usefulness. Consequently, some of the reported performance gains could stem from improved optimization toward reward-model preferences instead of broader alignment improvements.

That said, AAD may still be practically useful in scenarios where a preference-optimized model remains overly constrained by the SFT prior. By reducing the effective bias introduced by the KL regularization term, AAD can amplify preference signals at inference time. However, this mechanism also weakens safeguards that typically limit reward over-optimization, making the approach conceptually close to inference-time reward hacking.



Overall, while the method is interesting and computationally attractive, its alignment benefits would be more convincing if validated through stronger human evaluations or robustness tests beyond reward-model-based metrics.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
Does the evaluation setup favor reward-optimizing methods?AAD optimizes an implicit reward signal, and evaluation relies largely on reward models trained from similar preference data.

Does AAD amplify spurious reward signals? If the DPO model learned incorrect correlations, AAD could amplify them.

Does removing the implicit KL constraint during decoding introduce risks of reward over-optimization?
reviews.final_justification:
I summarize my updated assessment below:

(1) Concern about reward over-optimization:
The authors clarified that the method performs well under human evaluation. This partially addresses my concern, as strong human evaluation results suggest that the risk of reward over-optimization is likely limited.

(2) Concern about removing the implicit KL constraint:
The rebuttal provides additional explanation regarding the mechanism that prevents the model from drifting too far away from the SFT model’s regularization. I find the token filtering mechanism based on the α values plausible, although it may not fully resolve the concern.

Overall, the rebuttal improves my confidence in the paper to some extent. However, some concerns remain. As pointed out by another reviewer, the paper lacks evaluation on general capabilities, which raises the question of whether alignment improvements may come at the cost of general performance.

reviews.summary: This paper introduces Alignment-Aware Decoding (AAD), an inference-time method designed to improve alignment in language models trained with Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). AAD leverages the token-level log-likelihood ratio between the DPO aligned model and its supervised fine-tuned (SFT) reference model as a decoding-time alignment signal. This ratio is used to identify tokens that are more strongly preferred by the aligned policy than by the original SFT model, thereby steering generation toward responses that better reflect learned human preferences.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
### Strength
- The empirical results are strong and fairly broad for alignment-style evaluation, across multiple datasets and model families.

- The method remains competitive even when training data is limited.

### Weakness
- AAD requires access to both the aligned model and its original SFT reference model, and it doubles the number of per-token forward passes. The paper acknowledges this limitation, but it still reduces the method's practicality in deployment settings.

- Each decoding step requires two forward passes, one for the DPO model and one for the SFT model. The paper claims the method is more efficient than naive decoding, but latency analysis would strengthen this claim.

- The evaluation is convincing on preference-based alignment metrics, but it seems are dominated by reward-model scores, pairwise win rates, and AlpacaEval. The paper does not report standard benchmarks such as MMLU, GSM8K, or TruthfulQA, nor other reasoning evaluations measured by deterministic automatic metrics (e.g., accuracy or exact match).

- The method introduces a decoding hyperparameter $\alpha$. Although the paper provides an ablation study, the optimal range appears somewhat task- or model-dependent. It would be helpful to better understand how sensitive the method is in practice and whether $\alpha$ transfers reliably across datasets and model families.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
- Could the authors report latency or throughput numbers relative to greedy decoding and other baselines?

- Have the authors evaluated AAD on benchmarks such as MMLU, GSM8K, TruthfulQA, or other reasoning tasks to assess its performance on standard capability metrics such as accuracy?

- How sensitive is AAD to the choice of $\alpha$ across different tasks, datasets, and model families? Is there a robust $\alpha$ that works well generally?
reviews.final_justification:
The authors' rebuttal successfully addressed some of my concerns, particularly regarding (1) computational cost and (2) alpha sensitivity.

However, I still have reservations about the reliance on certain automatic evaluation metrics. In particular, the authors argue that this issue reflects an evaluation mismatch, stating that "We view this not as a limitation of AAD, but as an evaluation mismatch: the right way to evaluate a decoding method is on tasks that use decoding." I am not fully convinced by this justification. 

It is well known that decoding-based models (e.g., Qwen, GPT-OSS, etc.) perform well on these tasks, so I believe the issue may not be solely due to an evaluation mismatch.

Additionally, the rebuttal does not provide supporting references for this claim, which makes it difficult to assess its validity.

Overall, while the rebuttal clarified some aspects, it did not fully resolve my main concerns. Therefore, I lean toward a weak reject.

reviews.summary: The paper introduces Alignment-Aware Decoding (AAD), an inference-time alignment method that uses the log-likelihood ratio between a DPO-trained model and its SFT reference as a token-level reward signal.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Soundness: 
The paper is solid and the empirical results are convincing. The theoretical motivation is also clear. The experimental setup seems adequate.
I have a few concerns:

1. Table 6 presents results combining PPO with AAD. Is this experiment discussed in the main text? The results seem mixed and I believe this deserves explicit acknowledgment and discussion rather than being left in the appendix.

2. The iterative DPO experiments are interesting and the data-scarcity angle is compelling. However, these experiments are only conducted on a single dataset and model family. Could you replicate these findings with other datasets and model families?

3. The method should be significantly slower than greedy decoding, right? Could you quantify this? And it would be good to have this compared to other methods, not only greedy decoding.


Presentation: 
The paper is generally well-written and easy to follow. The background section is quite detailed; some of the information there could be condensed or moved to the appendix to improve flow. Table 1 has a lot of numbers, I'd think of other ways to present these results to improve readability.

Significance: 
The paper addresses an important problem. If the runtime cost can be properly characterized, this could be a good method for alignment. I suggest the authors discuss this a bit more.

Originality: 
The main idea is closely related to contrastive decoding, and the authors are transparent about this connection. However, the method is both practically well-motivated and theoretically supported. Overall, I think the contribution is novel enough to stand on its own.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors: I've asked a few questions (and made some recommendations) in the soundness point above.
reviews.final_justification: I increased my final recommendation to 5. My original concerns were addressed in the rebuttal and in their reply after my follow-up questions.
